did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) layers built on a ResNet-50 backbone to perform multiclass skin lesion classification on high-resolution dermoscopic images. The study evaluates this deep learning model on public dermatological datasets (ISIC 2018, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and PH2) to obtain classification accuracy, F1-scores, and AUC values for melanoma and other skin lesion detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a CNN-ASPP-based framework for skin lesion classification. It details the methodology (ResNet-50 backbone, ASPP layers), describes the evaluation on specific public datasets (ISIC 2018, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, PH2), and reports original quantitative results including accuracy, F1-scores, and AUC values. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCR 2025), it contains new technical contributions and empirical validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Lesion Classification in High-Resolution Dermoscopic Images Using CNNs with Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling Layers
brief_summary: This study proposes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework integrated with Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) layers built on a ResNet-50 backbone to perform multiclass skin lesion classification on high-resolution dermoscopic images. The model was evaluated on four public dermatological datasets: ISIC 2018, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and PH2. The primary evaluation approach involved measuring classification accuracy, F1-scores, and Area Under the Curve (AUC) values to assess diagnostic performance and generalization. The proposed CNN-ASPP architecture achieved final testing accuracies of 96.8% on ISIC 2018 and 91.4% on ISIC 2020, with an F1-score of 88.2% and AUC values exceeding 0.93 on key datasets. These results represent a 4.8–6.3% improvement in accuracy over baseline ResNet-50 models without ASPP, demonstrating enhanced capability in capturing multi-scale contextual features for melanoma and other lesion detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary malignant class studied; specifically highlighted for sensitivity improvements and detection in ISIC and PH2 datasets. Included in 7-class and binary classification tasks.
Malignant class studied; noted for high sensitivity in the CNN-ASPP model. Listed as a class in ISIC 2018 (abbreviated as BKL in Table VIII) and HAM10000 datasets.
Benign class studied; distinguished from malignancies. Listed as 'BKL' in ISIC 2018 results and 'SEB' in PH2 results. Figure 9 illustrates detected seborrheic keratosis lesions.
Benign class studied; Figure 8 illustrates detected nevus lesions. Included in HAM10000 (NV) and PH2 (NEV) datasets.
Rare lesion class studied; mentioned as having improved sensitivity with ASPP. Included in HAM10000 dataset.
Rare lesion class studied; included in HAM10000 dataset.
Rare lesion class studied; mentioned as benefiting from ASPP integration. Included in HAM10000 dataset (AKIEC).
Binary classification target in ISIC 2020 dataset (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma).

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Actinic Keratosis) and binary labels (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass skin lesion classification (7-class) and binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma). The model predicts discrete lesion classes using a CNN-ASPP architecture. Evaluation metrics include classification accuracy, F1-score, and AUC. The target is categorical label selection via softmax probability thresholds.

use.testing: Used for testing; achieved 96.8% accuracy and 0.91 AUC.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure stability across lesion types and dataset imbalance during evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.method: Probability threshold (≥0.5) for binary classification; Softmax for multiclass
statistical_methods_used.purpose: For label selection in binary and multiclass lesion outputs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper relies on descriptive performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC) and comparative analysis against baselines without formal statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018.notes: The model was evaluated on 7-class classification (NV, MEL, BKL). ASPP integration yielded a +5.1% accuracy improvement over the baseline. The model exhibited high sensitivity for melanoma and BCC.

HAM10000.notes: Evaluated on 7-class classification (MEL, NV, DF, VASC). ASPP integration yielded a +5.1% accuracy improvement over the baseline. Improved melanoma sensitivity by 21.6% over baseline CNN models.

PH2.notes: Evaluated on 7-class classification (MEL, NEV, SEB). ASPP integration yielded a +5.2% accuracy improvement over the baseline. AUC of 0.94 outperformed typical CNN baselines which often struggle to exceed 0.89.

ISIC 2020.notes: Evaluated on binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma). ASPP integration yielded a +4.8% accuracy improvement over the baseline. The model achieved an F1-score of 0.882 on the test set.

General Testing Set.notes: The proposed framework achieved a 4.8–6.3% gain in accuracy over standard CNNs. The model showed better minority class sensitivity and 2.3x less training epochs to convergence compared to baselines.

architecture_used.main_model: CNN-ASPP (Convolutional Neural Network with Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; models evaluated on public datasets (ISIC 2018, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, PH2) with standard backbones (ResNet-50, ResNet-101, Xception, DenseNet-121).

aspp_configuration.description: Multi-scale atrous convolutions integrated into the backbone to capture multi-scale context information without elevating computational load.

additional_modules: Spatial Fusion (used with ResNet-101 on ISIC 2020)
ASPP Decoder (used with Xception on HAM10000)

feature_processing.fusion_method: Channel-wise fusion of multi-scale features from ASPP branches

training_details.optimizer: SGD (Stochastic Gradient Descent) with dynamic learning rate schedules

classification_or_output_head.classes: 7-class (NV, MEL, BKL, DF, VASC, SEB, AKIEC) or Binary (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma)

Plain ResNet-50 with Global Avg Pooling
Unet++ with Standard Base CNN + Encoders

conclusions: The proposed CNN-ASPP framework, integrating multi-scale atrous convolutions into ResNet-50 and other backbones, significantly improves skin lesion classification accuracy over standard CNN baselines, achieving a 4.8–6.3% gain in accuracy.
The model demonstrated high diagnostic performance across diverse public datasets, achieving final testing accuracies of 96.8% on ISIC 2018, 91.4% on ISIC 2020, 92.3% on PH2, and 89.2% on HAM10000.

ASPP integration enhances the capture of multi-scale contextual features, leading to improved sensitivity for melanoma and rare lesion classes (such as Dermatofibroma and Actinic Keratosis) compared to baseline models.
The model exhibits strong generalization capabilities, with AUC values exceeding 0.93 on key datasets (ISIC 2020, PH2), outperforming typical CNN baselines which often struggle to exceed 0.89.

The architecture offers computational efficiency, requiring 2.3 times fewer training epochs to convergence than baselines and maintaining real-time inference feasibility with less than 170ms average latency on RTX-series GPUs.

The system is modular and scalable, allowing for easy integration into dermatological diagnostic workflows and fine-tuning for new lesion types, supporting AI-assisted skin cancer triage and screening.
important_remarks: The paper is published in a conference proceedings (ICCR 2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may affect the rigor of the peer review process.

The study aggregates data from multiple public datasets (ISIC 2018, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, PH2) but does not report specific class-wise sample counts or detailed demographic distributions, limiting assessment of population bias.
Performance metrics are reported as single point estimates (e.g., 96.8% accuracy) without confidence intervals or standard deviations, preventing assessment of statistical significance or variance.
The paper claims a 4.8–6.3% accuracy gain over baselines but does not provide p-values or formal statistical hypothesis testing to confirm the significance of these improvements.
The model was evaluated on public benchmark datasets; there is no evidence of external validation on independent, clinical-grade datasets from different institutions or geographic regions.

The paper mentions using 'entropy-based weighting and PROMETHEE & VIKOR methods' for model selection (Fig 3), but the specific application and impact of these Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) methods on the final results are not clearly detailed in the text.

The study claims real-time inference feasibility (<170ms latency) on RTX-series GPUs, but does not report performance on edge devices or mobile hardware, limiting generalizability to clinical deployment scenarios.

The paper does not explicitly discuss potential data leakage risks between the aggregated training sets or how class imbalance was handled beyond 'balanced augmentation', which is critical for rare lesion types like Dermatofibroma and Vascular Lesions.
